titleStmt.title: "Title of Work"
Glosses
titleStmt.funder: American Philosophical Society, Beinecke Library, Bibliographical Society of America, Blossman Foundation, Huntington Library, Louisiana Board of Regents, National Endowment for the Humanities, NINES, Pierpont Morgan Library, South Central Modern Language Association, and Southeastern Louisiana University

distributor.name: Southeastern Louisiana University Department of English

sourceDesc.p: Born digitally, prepared by David C. Hanson.

div.note: The battle of Trafalgar, a famous naval fought on October 21, 1805 during Nepoleonic wars. This poems honors the battle and its hero Nelson.
The personification echoes Southey's romantic vision of sea as both majestic and maternal.
The sea is shown as large and powerful. Southey also begins his story with the British ship moving proudly over the Ocean.
Many sailors will die before the day ends. Like Southey, the poet also shows both courage and loss- the high cost of victory.
The battle starts with noise and smoke. Southey also describes the fight as full of fire and thunder.
The sea turns red with blood. Southey also uses a similar image when he says the water "ran blood".

The British win but Nelson is killed. This matches Southey’s story where Nelson dies after victory. There lies an irony that Britain wins but the greatest hero fails. He also has a moral balance between triumph and tragedy.
